source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 13.1.1 IPv6 addressing (pp. 225-226)

questions.q: How is a preferred-format IPv6 address written?
options: As eight groups of four hexadecimal digits, separated by colons (X:X:X:X:X:X:X:X)
As four groups of decimal numbers separated by dots
As six hex bytes separated by colons
questions.why: IPv6 is 128 bits shown as eight colon-separated groups of four hex digits (e.g. 2001:0db8:130F:0000:0000:09C0:876A:130B).

questions.q: What is the rule for using the double colon (::) in an IPv6 address?
options: It replaces one or more consecutive all-zero groups, and may appear only ONCE in an address
It can appear as many times as needed
It replaces the leading zero of every group
It separates the network from the host like a slash
questions.why: :: stands in for consecutive zero groups but may be used only once, otherwise the address would be ambiguous.

questions.q: Abbreviate 2001:0db8:130F:0000:0000:09C0:876A:130B by omitting leading zeros only.
questions.why: Omitting leading zeros gives 2001:db8:130F:0:0:9C0:876A:130B; applying :: for the two zero groups would further shorten it to 2001:db8:130F::9C0:876A:130B.

questions.q: In IPv6, what are the equivalents of IPv4's network bits and host bits?
options: Network prefix (n bits) and Interface ID (128-n bits)
questions.why: The network prefix occupies n bits (like the network bits) and the interface ID the remaining 128-n bits (like host bits).

questions.q: How is an IPv6 address written in a URL to avoid confusion with the port?
options: With the port before the address
questions.why: IPv6 addresses are enclosed in square brackets in a URL so the trailing :port is unambiguous.
